title: Eva series is dark so why isnt the merchandise
display_title: Eva series is dark so why isnt the merchandise
wikitext:
[]: Eva series is dark so why isn't the merchandise?             Quote Post by Dataprime » Wed Oct 23, 2013 1:43 pm When you look at the other merchandise in the series; the games/ the toys/ the spinoff mangas etc. everything has a 'upbeat' and 'cheerful' feel to it when the show itself (and the movies) are pretty dark and deal with issues that many anime or other forums mediums just won't deal with. So why big this contrast going from a dark/twisted series to a almost lighthearted fan apparel & merchandise is it simply a way to get more $$$ out of eva fans to buy the latest Rei in bikini figure or a new chibi Shinji/Misato stickers?

[]:             Quote Post by Stillborn » Wed Oct 23, 2013 1:57 pm Of course its money. If fans feel strongly attached to characters they are more likely to get their hands on "happier" versions of them, than broken, tortured shells they are originally. Unless that is someone's kink.

[]:             Quote Post by The Eva Monkey » Wed Oct 23, 2013 2:07 pm Japan likes cute shit.

[]:             Quote Post by FreakyFilmFan4ever » Wed Oct 23, 2013 2:56 pm Marketing for TV anime isn't the same as marketing for an American Hollywood film. Anime's generally low-budget beginnings allowed it to be rather pulp-fiction in both story atmosphere and business practices, which is different from what we're used to with Hollywood. See, when Hollywood says "Target Audience," they really mean "Focus Groups" and "Test Screenings," where the audience is screened an unfinished version of the film. The audience will then be given a questionnaire on the film they just watched, where viewers write in how they felt about the film, and what they did or did not like. These responses are then given to the studios or networks, who will then make changes to the film based on the viewer's responses as well as decide how to better market and merchandise the film to it's audience. This generally means that the merchandising will mirror the original product in tone and IQ level. (I was a part of a test screening for a documentary on the rise and fall of the Palmetto Pointe TV show. I'm not sure what happened to the documentary after that.) In Japan, on the other hand, "Target Audience" for television anime producers means "If people will watch this or buy this, then we will make it." This attitude in mass producing without intense focus groups or test screenings results in merchandising from a show that doesn't always fit with the tone and IQ level of the original product. But at the same time it also results in a lot of unique shows that don't fit in with Western conventional ideas of genre or target audience. (As unique or strange as the shows may be, though, not all of them are worth watching.) Though this is true with a lot of animated television shows in Japan, it's not always true with original feature length films in Japan that don't have the success or popularity of a cheap TV show to back it up. If you want more information about that, you should check out Miyazaki's experience in his early years of being a feature film director before he founded Studio Ghibli.

[]:             Quote Post by Dataprime » Wed Oct 23, 2013 3:17 pm FreakyFilmFan4ever wrote: Marketing for TV anime isn't the same as marketing for an American Hollywood film. []:             Quote Post by Princess Asuka » Wed Oct 23, 2013 4:10 pm The Eva Monkey wrote: Japan likes cute shit.

[]:             Quote Post by AR-99 » Wed Oct 23, 2013 4:13 pm ^If you consider the Angels villains, Sachiel and Zeruel have gotten the most attention figurewise. Sachiel has even been turned into a cute, chibi-like version. And of course, there's all the Kaworu merch. The Eva-05 series has also gotten its share of figures and kits. These examples may be more in line with what the OP had in mind: *There's a game, and I can't recall which, but some of the NPCs like Gendo or Kaji I think get killed in somewhat grisly ways. *2 figures by Kaiyodo: Eva-01 - The Hard Core Mode comes with a battered, torso-only version of Sachiel and the Eva-05 Ultimate Ver. comes with a torso-only, partially consumed version of Eva-02.

[]:             Quote Post by Chuckman » Wed Oct 23, 2013 4:47 pm It's not limited to Japan. In the United States there was for years a series of toys based on a movie where alien monsters are born by literally tearing a hole in people's chests and crawling out and a movie about a killer robot from the future whose first action on screen is to impale Bill Paxton with his naked fist. Not to mention that we've been making toys out of horrible things like warfare forever. The answer is because people prefer to buy cute things. Sachiel gets so much merch because everybody likes the non-threatening jobber villain.

[]:             Quote Post by Dream » Wed Oct 23, 2013 6:37 pm FreakyFilmFan4ever wrote: Though this is true with a lot of animated television shows in Japan, it's not always true with original feature length films in Japan that don't have the success or popularity of a cheap TV show to back it up. If you want more information about that, you should check out Miyazaki's experience in his early years of being a feature film director before he founded Studio Ghibli.
